resources.string: Pick a photo to check for C2PA / Content Authenticity data — who made it, whether it has been edited or AI-generated, and whether the signer is trusted. You can also share a photo to this app from anywhere.
This photo has no embedded C2PA / Content Authenticity data.

This image\'s Content Credentials declare it was generated by AI — produced by a trained or algorithmic model rather than captured from a real scene. Edits made afterwards may have used AI too; see the deep-dive for the full history.

This image\'s Content Credentials declare it is a composite that includes AI-generated content — real content modified with AI (for example a generative-fill edit), rather than a wholly AI-generated image. The deep-dive shows the editing history.

The signer\'s certificate was reported as revoked, via an OCSP response stapled into the signature. A revoked certificate means the signing key should no longer be trusted — even if the signature is otherwise intact — so by default this image is treated as untrusted. (The app does not perform live revocation lookups; this only appears when the signer stapled a revoked OCSP response.)
This image\'s Content Credentials declare it was captured by a device such as a camera — a digital or computational capture of a real scene, rather than created or edited in software.

This image\'s Content Credentials declare it was created in software using non-generative tools — for example digital painting, graphic design, or a screenshot — rather than captured by a camera or generated by AI.
An algorithm was used to enhance this image — for example sharpening, noise reduction, or upscaling. This is computational processing, not generative AI.

This image was edited after it was originally captured or created — for example cropping, colour adjustments, or retouching. These are ordinary edits, not AI generation. See the deep-dive for the full editing history.
Captured by a camera or scanner
Created in software, not a camera
The steps recorded in this image\'s history. Tap a step to learn what it means.
No actions were recorded in this image\'s Content Credentials.
This step was recorded in the image\'s Content Credentials as part of its history.
The image was first created — this is the origin of the file. The source (camera, software, or AI) is shown alongside.
An existing file was opened in an editor, starting a new round of edits.
Another image or asset was imported and placed into this one (for example a layer or a pasted element).
Part of the content was removed or deleted.
The image was edited. This is a general edit; more specific steps (like cropping or colour) may also be listed.
The image was cropped — its edges were trimmed to change the framing.
Colours, brightness, contrast, or similar tones were adjusted.
Content was drawn or painted onto the image by hand.
A filter or effect was applied to the whole image.
The image was resized or scaled to different dimensions.
The image was rotated or flipped.
A watermark was added to the image.
Metadata (such as captions, location, or other embedded info) was edited.
Information was redacted — hidden or blacked out — from the content.
The image was converted to a different format or colour space.
The image data was re-encoded, often to a different format or compression.
The file was reformatted or repackaged without changing the visible content.
The image was published or distributed.
The asset was managed by a system (for example imported into or tracked by a library).
The image was produced or rendered into its final form.
An action was recorded, but its type wasn\'t specified by the tool that made it.
No custom rules yet. Open a photo\'s details to allow or deny its signer.
C2PA Verify reads Content Credentials embedded in a photo — the signer, the tool that created it, and when it was signed.
Know if you can trust it
A bold banner tells you at a glance whether the photo is signed and trusted, signed by an unknown party, or tampered with. AI-generated images are flagged.
Pick a photo or share one to C2PA Verify from any app. Manage which authorities you trust, then export a report with the verdict laid over the image.
